rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the policy mechanism even if an outcome token is missing. The policy statement targets waste prevention and reuse: 'reduce use of non-reusable foodware' and 'promoting reusables for on and off-site dining.' This aligns with Resource Efficiency (reduction/reuse of materials) and supports the listed mechanism (avoiding emissions from single-use production and waste disposal). climate_screen=explicit_self breaks the tie in favor of keeping.
policy_statement: By 2023, reduce use of non-reusable foodware by requiring/incentivizing/supporting/promoting reusables for on- and off-site dining (to-go/delivery) (RPC.3-1).
mechanism_description: It reduces non-reusable foodware by requiring, incentivizing, supporting and/or promoting reusable options, which cuts disposable waste sent to landfill and associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same food-service function → ↓ material throughput (single-use items) → ↓ upstream production + ↓ waste treatment emissions → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system/activity: Food service and consumption practices shift from disposable/single-use cups, clamshells, cutlery, etc. toward durable, reusable foodware via requirements and/or enabling reuse systems (return, wash, redistribution).  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced demand for virgin material extraction and manufacturing of disposables; reduced frequency/volume of waste collection and treatment (landfill/incineration/composting), with any reusables’ washing impacts typically lower per use when reuse cycles are sufficient and systems are efficient.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions (CO₂e), including avoided production emissions and (where applicable) avoided landfill methane and combustion emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle GHG emissions from manufacturing and disposing of fewer single-use foodware items due to a shift to reusables.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Delivering the same dining/takeout service with fewer single-use products reduces material throughput, which most strongly reduces upstream production emissions (and also reduces waste-management emissions).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “landfill methane avoidance” is mentioned, foodware is often inert (plastics/metals) or only partly methane-generating, and the larger, more generalizable effect across all such policies is avoided manufacturing and disposal of disposables—i.e., resource efficiency via waste prevention/reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy targeted specifically compostable/food-contaminated items or landfill diversion as the primary lever, mitigation via landfill methane avoidance could become more salient; however, the shared mechanism here is replacement of single-use with reusables, whose dominant effect is reduced production/waste overall.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that reduces single-use foodware by promoting/requiring reusables necessarily reduces the quantity of disposable items produced and discarded, yielding the same resource-efficiency-driven emissions reduction pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific secondary categorization (e.g., whether the emissions benefit is mostly upstream manufacturing vs. landfill/incineration, and whether washing energy affects net outcomes) depends on policy design and local context and should be assigned at the individual policy evidence level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net climate benefit can vary with reuse system design (number of reuses achieved, washing energy source, transport/logistics). If the policy is implemented primarily as landfill diversion of methane-generating materials (e.g., contaminated paper/bioplastics to landfill avoidance), Mitigation could dominate; but for “single-use reduction via reusables,” resource-efficiency (waste prevention) is typically primary.
key_indicators: “By 2023”; “reduce use of non-reusable foodware”; “requiring, incentivizing, supporting and/or promoting reusables”; “on and off-site dining (to-go or delivery)”
co_benefits: Reduced litter and plastic pollution; lower solid waste management burdens/costs; potential water/air quality benefits from reduced incineration/landfill impacts
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument type is ambiguous because the text lists multiple possible approaches (require/incentivize/support/promote). Classified as target-setting because the clearest actionable policy element is the time-bound reduction objective, with instruments left open.
